cataloging_subject.label: The Sayings of St. Bernard and The Vision of St. Paul
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Carl Horstmann's 1874 two-poem editorial selection as completely encoded in CME00150, not one medieval authored work. Part I is the anonymous Middle English Sayings of St. Bernard, a mortality and penitential poem loosely adapting Pseudo-Bernardian material and preserved in several versions. Part II is the anonymous Middle English Vision of St. Paul, a unique Laud version descending from the fourth redaction of the Latin Visio Pauli. Both are six-line stanzaic poems copied consecutively by Laud Misc. 108 Hand A, but they retain separate titles, incipits, DIMEV identities, source traditions, witness distributions, dates, and subjects. Horstmann's German title, byline, headings, and notes form a third editorial layer rather than part of either medieval work.

catalog_summary.title: The Sayings of St. Bernard and The Vision of St. Paul
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous Middle English poet-adapters; The Sayings is traditionally associated with Bernard of Clairvaux
catalog_summary.date: The Sayings has a joint uncertain first-attestation window of 1250-1282 from Add. E. 6 (dated 1250-1300) and Digby 86 (c. 1272-1282); the Laud Vision is attested in Hand A around 1300; Horstmann's editorial selection was published in 1874
catalog_summary.region: England; exact places of composition are unknown, and Laud Hand A's West Oxfordshire scribal dialect is retained only as witness-level evidence

scope.description: Two Middle English poems in 73 six-line stanzas; brief modern editorial framing does not convert the literary selection to mixed form.

form_statements.description: Both poems use six-line aabccb stanzas. CME00150 encodes 31 stanzas / 186 lines for the Sayings and 42 stanzas / 252 lines for the Vision.
Modern German title, byline, headings, and compact prose notes.

assertion.notes: Both derive from Latin traditions, but neither immediate physical exemplar has been identified.

subject_statements.label: Mortality, bodily decay, confession, and penance
Body and soul; the World, Flesh, and Devil
Ubi sunt, worldly vanity, wealth, and unjust possession
Hell, postmortem punishment, and the classification of sins
Sunday respite for souls in hell
Paul's guided vision with Michael and intercession for the damned
Penance, divine mercy, judgment, and salvation

summaries.value: A two-poem editorial selection from Laud Misc. 108. The Sayings of St. Bernard urges self-knowledge and penance through reminders of bodily decay, conflict between flesh and soul, the three enemies of humankind, worldly vanity, unjust possessions, and death, ending with an appeal to Mary. The Vision of St. Paul follows Paul and Michael through punishments in hell, connects penalties to particular sins, recounts the removal of a soul to damnation and the reception of a good soul, and culminates in Christ granting the damned respite from Saturday noon until Monday morning before a closing prayer for grace at judgment. Horstmann prints both anonymous six-line stanzaic works directly from adjacent Laud leaves with German headings and textual notes.

Thirty-one six-line stanzas in the Laud version. The speaker invokes what Saint Bernard says in his book, meditates on death and bodily corruption, urges the soul to master the flesh and resist the World, Flesh, and Devil, condemns pride and unjust wealth, deploys an ubi sunt sequence, commends suffering and penitential discipline, and closes with prayer to Mary. Laud lacks the first stanza found in other versions but is not physically defective at the opening.

Forty-two six-line stanzas and 252 lines, uniquely witnessed in Laud Misc. 108. Michael guides Paul through a highly abbreviated vision of hell: hanging souls, a beast-filled river and narrow bridge, graduated depths of punishment, sealed pits, seven-colored fire, penalties for sexual, verbal, religious, and homicidal sins, and contrasting departures of a damned and a saved soul. Paul and Michael intercede, Christ grants the Sunday respite, and the poem closes by stressing hell's innumerable pains and praying to see Christ at judgment.

CME00150 must not be assigned one author, one DIMEV identifier, one source tradition, or one first-attestation date. The two poems are adjacent and formally coordinated in Laud and Horstmann's article, but DIMEV 5215 has a multi-witness Sayings tradition whereas DIMEV 4814 is a unique Laud Vision version; Pseudo-Bernardian Meditationes and Latin Visio Pauli redaction IV are likewise separate upstream work relationships. German editorial prose and the 1874 date remain edition-layer metadata.

extent.description: Two numbered poem divisions: The Sayings of St. Bernard has 31 LG stanza groups and 186 L verse lines; The Vision of St. Paul has 42 LG stanza groups and 252 L verse lines. Each stanza has six lines, for 73 stanzas and 438 lines total. The separate German editorial-frame part has title, byline, headings, and ten encoded notes but is not counted as a third literary item.

notes: Canvas 413 is a shared boundary surface: the Sayings ends at f. 199rb and the Vision begins there.
Laud omits the first stanza present in other versions. The current Bodleian catalog explicitly says this is not evidence of accidental physical imperfection.

DIMEV 5215 lists five witnesses with different extents: Add. E. 6 (first poem on a roll), Digby 86 (ff. 125v-127r), Laud 108 (ff. 198ra-199rb), Harley 2253 (ff. 106ra-107rb), and the Auchinleck fragment (f. 280ra, final seven stanzas).

Monda treats Add. E. 6 and Digby 86 as the two earliest copies and argues that Add. E. 6 is structurally closer to the prototype while preserving rougher copy. This remains an attributed 1970 editorial judgment, not a resolved stemma or proof of composition region.
Ker 1965 is a partial modern photographic facsimile that includes the Harley target. Its physical purchase and consultation routes do not establish a public digital copy or an open reuse license.
DIMEV 5215's author field is not imported as verified Bernardine authorship.

related_works.title: Meditationes piissimae de cognitione humanae conditionis
notes: The Sayings loosely adapts mortality and devotional material associated with this Pseudo-Bernardian Latin tradition. Brent does not identify a physical exemplar or a simple line-by-line translation relationship.
This relation is scoped to part:sayings-of-st-bernard only.

related_works.title: Visio Pauli, fourth Latin redaction
notes: The Vision is a highly abbreviated descendant of the fourth redaction, itself part of the wider Apocalypse of Paul tradition; its immediate exemplar is unknown.
This relation is scoped to part:vision-of-st-paul only.

related_works.title: Vernon-version Sayings of St. Bernard
notes: DIMEV and Brent treat this as essentially the same poem without two introductory stanzas, but exact stemmatic and work-boundary decisions remain open.
This relation is scoped to part:sayings-of-st-bernard only.

related_works.title: Dispute between the Body and the Soul in Laud Misc. 108
notes: The following poem completes Brent's three-text eschatological cluster but is not included in CME00150. It begins on the same f. 200v / canvas 416 surface where the Vision ends.

evidence.summary: Confirms the two poem divisions, titles and byline, Middle English text, German editorial layer, incipits and explicits, notes, page and folio milestones, stanza grouping, and repository identifier.

Counted one DIV1, two DIV2, 73 LG, 438 L, six PB, six MILESTONE, and ten NOTE elements; assigned 31 stanzas / 186 lines / four notes to the Sayings and 42 stanzas / 252 lines / six notes to the Vision.

evidence.citation: Linked lineage evidence for J. Justin Brent's 2011 chapter on the Laud eschatological cluster.

open_questions.question: What work-level and stemmatic boundaries best describe DIMEV 5215, DIMEV 4564, the Laud version, and the other later-thirteenth- and fourteenth-century Sayings witnesses?
next_steps: Collate every stanza and sequence across Add. E.6, Digby 86, Laud 108, Harley 2253, the Auchinleck fragment, and Vernon-version witnesses.
Test Brent's observation of four Laud-specific stanzas without presuming Hand A authored them.
Create separate related-work identities only after distinguishing versions, excerpts, and accidental loss.

open_questions.question: Which exact Latin or vernacular exemplar and branch of Visio Pauli redaction IV underlie the Laud Vision, and how much of its abbreviation and six-line shaping belongs to the surviving copyist or an earlier adapter?
next_steps: Collate the 252 lines with current critical texts and manuscripts of redaction IV.
Map inherited episodes, Sunday Respite material, omissions, expansions, and stanza-level restructuring.
Do not turn the unique surviving witness into proof of unique composition or direct translation from a named physical exemplar.

open_questions.question: Was the unique Vision version composed or revised specifically to follow the Sayings and participate in the Laud eschatological cluster?
next_steps: Compare formulae, stanza transitions, dialect, corrections, and thematic sequencing across the Sayings, Vision, and following Dispute.
Separate Hand A copying, possible local revision, and pre-existing exemplar structure.
Retain the cluster relationship as contextual rather than authorial until stronger evidence emerges.

open_questions.question: Can either poem's place of composition be established independently of Laud Hand A's West Oxfordshire scribal dialect and the manuscript's tentative Oxford production history?
next_steps: Use the multi-witness Sayings tradition to separate authorial language from later scribal localization.
Compare the unique Vision with Hand A's broader linguistic profile and its source tradition.
Keep place facets broad until composition evidence exists.

notes: The XML TEXT/@LANG value ‘enm ger’ accurately describes the encoded edition's language layers but does not make German a language of either medieval poem.
Horstmann's title footnote cites Skeat 1868 Preface p. xxxv for Madden's English labels. Skeat is a supporting citation, not an intermediary source for either transcription.
The Sayings' joint uncertain 1250-1282 first-attestation window and the Vision's c. 1300 Laud attestation must not be collapsed into one date for the edited selection.

The underlying medieval works and Latin source traditions, five Sayings witnesses, Laud Vision witness, historical and modern editions, U-M encoding, repository artifact, photographs, catalog data, DIMEV release, and Brill scholarship retain separate descriptive and rights layers.
